title: Serverless and Functions as a Service
description:
## Code That Runs Only When Something Happens

**Serverless** computing lets you run code without provisioning or managing servers. There *are* still servers — but the provider owns them entirely, allocating capacity automatically per request. The most common form is **Functions as a Service (FaaS)**: you upload a function, the platform runs it in response to events, and you are billed only for the time it executes.

### The platforms

| Platform | Provider |
|---|---|
| **AWS Lambda** | Amazon Web Services |
| **Azure Functions** | Microsoft Azure |
| **Google Cloud Functions** | Google Cloud |
| **Cloudflare Workers** | Cloudflare — runs at the edge, close to users |
| **Vercel / Netlify Functions** | Frontend-focused platforms, popular for JAMstack and API routes |

### Event-driven execution

Functions are triggered by **events** rather than running continuously:

- An HTTP request hits an API gateway
- A file is uploaded to object storage
- A message lands on a queue or stream
- A scheduled timer fires (cron-style)

```text
Upload image to S3  ->  triggers Lambda  ->  function generates a thumbnail  ->  writes result back
```

### Scale-to-zero and pay-per-invocation

When no events arrive, the platform runs **zero** instances and you pay **nothing** for compute. Under load it spins up many concurrent instances automatically. Billing is per **invocation** plus execution duration (e.g. GB-seconds), so idle workloads cost almost nothing — a sharp contrast with an always-on VM you pay for 24/7.

### Trade-offs

| Benefit | Cost / limitation |
|---|---|
| No server management | **Cold starts** — the first request after idle pays a startup delay while the runtime initializes |
| Scales to zero, pay only for use | **Execution limits** — max duration (often a few minutes), memory caps, payload size limits |
| Automatic scaling | **Vendor lock-in** — triggers, packaging, and APIs differ per provider; migrating is non-trivial |
| Fast to deploy small units of logic | Harder to debug, test locally, and trace across many small functions |

### When serverless fits

Serverless shines for spiky or unpredictable workloads, glue code between services, scheduled jobs, and lightweight APIs. It is a poor fit for long-running processes, latency-critical paths that cannot tolerate cold starts, or workloads needing persistent in-memory state.
